title: Toy Ice Cream Cart
summary: A rolling pretend-play ice cream cart with a striped awning, wheeled legs, and hinged serving flaps.
description:
This is a genuine little vehicle, not just a box on legs, and the wheel assembly is where that shows: a dedicated wheel-support system gets clamped and shimmed until the finished cart sits level before you ever drill through the wheel centers, because a cart with wheels bolted on crooked rolls or rocks no matter how square the box itself is. Build the box, trim, and awning as separate sub-assemblies first — the plan explicitly sets each one aside before moving to the next — and only bring them together once each piece is complete on its own.

The awning dowel and the serving-flap hinges are the details that sell the "ice cream cart" idea over a plain box on wheels. Hinge the 1x8 serving flaps to the 1x2 trim last, after the box and legs are fully assembled, since that's when you can actually check the flaps swing clear of the wheels and legs.

materials.name: Bolts, washers, and nuts
materials.note: Mounts the wheels to the wheel-support system.

cutList.note: Long point to long point, both ends cut at 45° off square, ends not parallel.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two box ends at 15", two box sides at 22-1/2" and two serving flaps at 16" from the Pine, 1x8. Cut one box bottom at 16-1/2" from the Plywood. Cut two box top trim at 30", one box end trim at 18", two awning trim at 16-3/4", one awning end trim at 13-1/2", two cross braces at 13" and one serving flap trim at 15" from the Pine, 1x2. Cut one umbrella pole at 16-1/2" from the Wood dowel. Cut one awning support dowel at 12" from the Wood dowel. Cut two legs at 40" from the Pine, 2x2. Cut one wheel support, top at 15", two wheel support, sides at 4" and one top piece at 16-1/2" from the Pine, 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 10 ft Pine, 1x8, two 8 ft Pine, 1x2, one 8 ft Pine, 2x2 and one 4 ft Pine, 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: The two 22-1/2" sides run the full length and the two 15" ends fit between them, which makes the box 22-1/2" long by 16-1/2" wide overall — exactly the size of the plywood bottom, so use the bottom as your squaring gauge rather than a tape. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of each 15" end piece and join with 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws, or glue and 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws if you would rather nothing showed inside a toy. Then lay the box down on the plywood bottom, pull it square to the panel's corners, and fasten the bottom on with 3/4" brad nails or staples every few inches.

steps.title: Trim the box and add the umbrella pole
steps.body: Hold each trim piece against the assembled box and mark it there rather than trusting the cut list on this one — the box finishes 22-1/2" x 16-1/2", and the listed 30" top trim and 18" end trim are longer than that box needs even with outward 45° miters at both ends, so they are either meant to run past the box or the numbers are off. Cut the miters, then glue and 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws nail the trim around the top edge. Drill for the 1-1/4" dowel umbrella pole and fasten it with 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws driven from inside the box, checking it stands plumb with a square from two directions before the screws go tight — the awning and everything above it hangs off this one post. Set the assembly aside.

steps.title: Build the awning
steps.body: The awning is its own small frame: the two 16-3/4" awning trim pieces with the 13-1/2" awning end trim across them, joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws, or glue and 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws. Build it flat on the bench and check its diagonals — it has to sit level across two legs later, and a racked frame will tilt the whole awning. Drill for the 12" support dowel and fasten it with screws so it spans the frame and gives the striped awning fabric something to drape over. No fabric is listed in the materials, so plan on supplying your own. Set the awning aside.

steps.title: Attach the awning to the legs
steps.body: Stand the two 40" 2x2 legs parallel on the bench and set the awning frame across their tops with the outside faces flush. Predrill, then drive 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws down through the awning frame into the end grain of each leg, two per joint — a 2x2 splits readily when screwed near the end, which is exactly what these screws are doing. Check the frame is square to both legs before the second screw at each joint goes in; this sub-assembly is what sets whether the finished cart stands upright or leans.

steps.title: Attach the legs to the box
steps.body: Stand the leg-and-awning assembly against one long side of the box. The legs are 40" and carry the box as well as the awning above it, so hold the box at the height you want the serving surface for the child who will use it, mark both legs at that line, and clamp or have a helper hold it there. Drive 2-3/4" screws from inside the box outward into each leg — driving from the inside keeps every fastener hidden on the finished cart. Two or three per leg is plenty. Sight the cart from the front and confirm it stands square before the last screws go tight, then set the assembly aside.

steps.title: Build the wheel support system
steps.body: Build the wheel support from the three 1x4 pieces: the 15" top with a 4" side hanging down at each end, making a shallow U. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws, or 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws and glue. The two 4" sides are what the wheel bolts will pass through, so get them parallel to each other and square to the top — if they are out of square the two 12" wheels end up on different axes and the cart crabs sideways instead of rolling straight, which no amount of care at the drilling stage will fix.

steps.title: Mount the wheels
steps.body: Clamp the wheels to the wheel-support system and check the whole cart sits level before drilling anything permanent. Drill through the center of each wheel into the support system and attach with bolts, washers, and nuts.

steps.title: Hinge the serving flaps
steps.body: Hinge the two 16" 1x8 serving flaps to the 15" 1x2 serving flap trim, setting the hinges in from each end of the flap so their screws land in solid wood well away from the end grain. Do this last, with the box, legs and wheels all assembled, because this is the only point at which you can swing each flap through its full arc and confirm it clears the wheels and the legs before the hinge screws are finally tightened. Predrill every hinge screw hole — 3/4" pine splits easily near an edge and a split at a hinge is not repairable. Sand every edge and corner a child will touch before it goes into service.

images.alt: Toy wooden ice cream cart with a striped awning, wheels, and hinged serving flaps
